program: Business English — Executive Communication for International Leadership
hub_subtitle: Business English Executivo para Reuniões Internacionais e Liderança

lesson.menu_desc: Reading a company profile and describing people, companies and markets -- relative clauses (defining and non-defining: who, which, that, where, whose) -- 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 12 -- Who's Who at AVRT -- Describing People, Companies and Markets
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Izabel Cristina de Pieri | Aula 12 -- Who's Who at AVRT

listenings.text: Hi Izabel, it is Philip, the investor who backed your first round. I am briefing a partner about AVRT, and I want to get the story right, so tell me if I am wrong. AVRT, which you founded in Valinhos back in 2015, is the company that makes the wireless sensors factories use to track equipment. You are the CEO whose background is in electronics, and the engineer who leads your research is the one whose designs won those two awards. What I like is the niche, which most big firms ignore: clients who need cutting-edge sensors and the proprietary software that runs them. The subsidiary that you opened this year, the one which handles North American sales, is the piece that got my partner's attention. A company that owns its technology and has a track record clients trust is exactly the kind of venture we want. Call me.

Good morning, Izabel. It is Paula, your market analyst, with a quick read on the competition. The segment you sell into, which is growing about fifteen percent a year, used to be dominated by one incumbent, the firm whose prices were always lower than yours. That is the company that everyone assumed would win. But the buyers who matter, the ones who run large factories, care about reliability, not just price. AVRT, which built its name on quality, is now the supplier they call first. Your flagship sensor, the product that started it all, is still the benchmark that rivals are measured against. My advice, which I will put in the note I circulate, is simple: keep telling the story of the mentor who guided you and the track record that clients trust. That is the message which sells. Talk soon.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: the person who started a company
the main office where a company is run
a company's most important product or service
a specialized segment of the market
the most advanced; state of the art
owned exclusively by one company; patented
the established company that already dominates a market
a history of past performance and achievements

paras: AVRT, which was founded in Valinhos in 2015, is a company that designs wireless sensors for industry. The founder who started it, Izabel, is a CEO whose background is in electronics. The product that made the company's name, its flagship sensor, is used by factories that need to track equipment in real time. It began as a small venture that grew into a serious player.

The headquarters where AVRT operates sits just outside Valinhos. The engineer who leads research, and whose designs won two awards, built a team that competes on quality. AVRT serves a niche which most large firms ignore: clients who need cutting-edge sensors and the proprietary software that runs them. The technology that AVRT owns is the reason customers stay.

Every company has stakeholders who care about its future: the investors who fund it, the employees who build it, and the clients who depend on it. AVRT recently opened a subsidiary, which handles its North American sales. The incumbent that once dominated the segment, a firm whose prices were always lower, is now the company AVRT is catching. A mentor who advised Izabel early on once said that a track record which clients trust is worth more than any advertisement.
reading.source: Adapted from an internal AVRT company profile

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: AVRT is a large multinational that dominates the global sensor market.
A profile of AVRT: who founded it, what it makes, the niche it serves, and how it is catching the incumbent.
AVRT is closing its subsidiary and leaving the sensor business.

items: AVRT was founded in Valinhos in 2015.
'AVRT, which was founded in Valinhos in 2015...'
Large firms compete heavily in the niche AVRT serves.
'AVRT serves a niche which most large firms ignore.'
AVRT opened a subsidiary that handles its North American sales.
'AVRT recently opened a subsidiary, which handles its North American sales.'
The incumbent's prices were always higher than AVRT's.
'The incumbent... a firm whose prices were always lower.'

parts: background is in electronics. The headquarters,
sits outside Valinhos, houses the R&D team. It is the place
the flagship sensor was designed. AVRT recently opened a subsidiary,

gapfill.text: Relative clauses add information about a noun. Defining clauses (no commas) tell us WHICH one and are essential: use who for people, which or that for things, where for places, whose for possession ('the engineer who leads R&D', 'the sensor that we make'). Non-defining clauses (with commas) add EXTRA information and use who or which, never 'that' ('AVRT, which was founded in 2015, ...').

bank.label: Useful language for describing people and companies
items: AVRT is a company that makes wireless sensors.
The founder who started it is Izabel.
Izabel is a CEO whose background is in electronics.
The headquarters, which sits outside Valinhos, houses the team.
It serves a niche that most large firms ignore.
The subsidiary, which handles North American sales, is new.

lf.text: Defining (no commas): who = people, which/that = things, where = places, whose = possession. The clause is essential and cannot be removed. Non-defining (with commas): who or which only, never 'that'; it adds extra, non-essential information. In defining clauses you can drop the pronoun when it is the object ('the deal we signed'), but never in non-defining clauses.

items: Describe AVRT in one sentence using a defining relative clause with 'that' or 'which'.
Describe Izabel using a relative clause with 'who' or 'whose'.
Add extra information about AVRT's headquarters using a non-defining clause (commas + 'which').
Describe one real person or company you work with, using at least two relative clauses.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = that, 2 = who, 3 = whose, 4 = which, 5 = where, 6 = which.
Scenario 1: 'AVRT is a company that makes wireless sensors.'
Scenario 2: 'Izabel is a CEO whose background is in electronics.'
Scenario 3: 'AVRT, which was founded in 2015, is based near Valinhos.'
answerkey.note: Defining clauses (no commas) use who/that/which/where/whose and are essential. Non-defining clauses (with commas) use who/which -- never 'that' -- and add extra information.
